commands.review_summary:
The PR adds a Windows-only `readBrowserVersion` path that reads PE ProductVersion through PowerShell, falls back to WMIC, and then preserves the existing `--version` probe.

PR surface: Source +28. Total +28 across 1 file.

Reproducibility: yes. at source level, but not live in this review: current main calls `readBrowserVersion` from Browser doctor and has no Windows metadata path when the generic `--version` probe returns null. The linked Windows 11 Chrome 148 ProductVersion report matches that path.

target.title: fix(browser): read Chrome version from PE metadata on Windows
target.body:
## Description

Fixes #87312

`openclaw doctor` reports "Could not determine the installed Chrome version" even when Chrome is installed at the expected path with correct version metadata (Chrome 148.x in this case).

## Root Cause

The `readBrowserVersion` function on Windows runs `chrome.exe --version` to determine the installed version. Launching Chrome this way can fail on Windows because:
- Chrome may attempt to show a GUI or conflict with an already-running instance
- The process startup can be slow and hit the timeout
- Chrome's Windows process model differs from macOS/Linux where `--version` returns immediately

## Fix

Added a Windows-specific code path that reads file version metadata directly from the PE executable using PowerShell (Get-Item).VersionInfo.ProductVersion, which:
- Is instant (no process launch)
- Works correctly with all Chrome versions including 148+
- Falls back to `wmic` if PowerShell is unavailable

The `chrome.exe --version` fallback remains as a last resort.

## Testing

- Windows 11 with Chrome 148.0.7778.179 at default install path
- `openclaw doctor` now correctly detects "Detected Chrome 148.0.7778.179"
- No change to macOS or Linux behavior
target.author: jefsky
